title: New York & Harlaem Railroad Omnibus
desc: Early octagonal New York & Harlaem Railroad omnibus token in copper.
notes:
The New York & Harlaem Railroad, incorporated in 1831, operated one of the earliest urban rail lines in the United States and is widely regarded as New York City’s first street railway. Service began in 1832 along the Bowery from Prince Street to 14th Street, with later extensions north on Fourth Avenue to Harlem and south to City Hall. Horse power was used initially, with steam locomotives introduced in 1837 for service north of the 27th Street depot. These tokens are believed to be the first transportation tokens issued for use in New York City.

Collectors recognize seven major octagonal token varieties associated with the line. The regular circulating issues are the 18mm German silver pieces listed as Atwood-Coffee NY630: plain (NY630Dc), rosette counterstamp (NY630Da), and the famous dog counterstamp (NY630Db). Additional pattern issues were struck in copper and lead under the NY998 listings. Copper varieties include plain (NY998Ca) and leaf counterstamp (NY998Cb). Lead varieties include plain (NY998Da) and leaf counterstamp (NY998Db). The initials 'B & S' found beneath the railroad coach identify the token makers, Bale & Smith, noted New York engravers.

Today these pieces are considered among the most historic American transit tokens, linking directly to the earliest development of organized public transportation in New York City.

title: Proposed pattern for the NYC subway.
obv: NEW YORK CITY TRANSIT SYSTEM NYC
notes: This was a proposed token for the NYC subway. This design was ultimately not used.
